metadata.summary: RFC 7807 Problem Details for HTTP APIs: standardized 4xx/5xx error envelope with type, title, status, detail, instance, traceId.

content.title: Error Handling (RFC 7807 Problem Details)
content.summary: Standardized HTTP API error envelope following RFC 7807 / RFC 9457: every 4xx and 5xx response carries type (URI), title, status, detail, instance, and traceId fields, with an optional errors[] array for field-level validation failures. A single exception handler per framework maps all error types to this shape.

why.p: Inconsistent error shapes break API clients, make debugging impossible without log access, and prevent automated SDK generation. RFC 7807 gives clients a machine-readable type URI to branch on, a stable traceId to correlate with backend traces, and a status field that matches the HTTP status — three properties that ad-hoc {"error": "..."} payloads never reliably provide.

list.item: Designing or refactoring HTTP API error responses so every endpoint emits the same envelope.
Mapping framework exceptions to HTTP responses (FastAPI, Express, Spring, ASP.NET, Axum).
Wiring traceId into responses and logs for Sentry/Datadog/Tempo correlation.
Generating OpenAPI/Problem schema components for SDK code-gen.
Migrating ad-hoc {"error": "..."} payloads to a documented error contract.
Internal RPC/gRPC paths — use google.rpc.Status instead.
WebSocket frames or SSE event streams — design a separate envelope.
Hot paths where every byte matters (mobile-bandwidth APIs) — strip to code/message.
Public unauthenticated endpoints where instance and traceId leak request topology.

rule.statement: Every 4xx and 5xx response must carry: type (URI identifying the error class), title (short human label, stable per type), status (integer matching HTTP status), detail (per-request human description), instance (request path), traceId (correlation ID from the tracing SDK).
rule.rationale: Consistent fields let clients, gateways, and monitoring tools parse errors without ad-hoc string matching. traceId is the critical field for correlating client-reported errors with server traces.

example.title: ProblemDetail JSON envelope with validation errors

section.name: openapi-component

rule.statement: Define ProblemDetail and FieldError as reusable OpenAPI components. Reference them in every 4xx and 5xx response of every operation. Use application/problem+json as the Content-Type, not application/json.
rule.rationale: A $ref keeps the schema in one place. Using application/problem+json lets proxies, gateways, and clients distinguish error responses from normal ones by Content-Type alone.

section.reference: OpenAPI components/schemas definitions for ProblemDetail and FieldError.

rule.statement: Define error codes as stable machine-readable slugs (snake_case, versioned per type URI). Codes must be stable across releases — renames break clients silently.
rule.rationale: Clients branch on codes, not on HTTP status alone (e.g., conflict vs duplicate-key vs optimistic-lock all return 409 but need distinct codes). Stable slugs let clients ship independent of server release cycles.

example.title: Error code enum definition
example.code: class ErrorCode: # Client Errors (4xx) VALIDATION_ERROR = "validation_error" INVALID_FORMAT = "invalid_format" MISSING_FIELD = "missing_field" UNAUTHORIZED = "unauthorized" FORBIDDEN = "forbidden" NOT_FOUND = "not_found" CONFLICT = "conflict" RATE_LIMITED = "rate_limited" # Server Errors (5xx) INTERNAL_ERROR = "internal_error" SERVICE_UNAVAILABLE = "service_unavailable" TIMEOUT = "timeout"

section.name: exception-handlers

rule.statement: Register three exception handlers: one for ValidationError, one for HTTPException, one for bare Exception (catch-all). Never omit the catch-all — unhandled exceptions must also return ProblemDetail with traceId so support can find the trace.
rule.rationale: Missing the catch-all breaks the error contract: the client receives a 500 with an HTML or empty body instead of ProblemDetail, and the traceId is lost.

section.reference: Full FastAPI ProblemDetail model and three exception handlers.

rule.statement: Set traceId from the active tracing SDK context (OpenTelemetry trace_id, Datadog dd.trace_id), not from uuid.uuid4(). The traceId in the body must match the trace ID in server logs.
rule.rationale: A freshly generated UUID in the response body never matches any log entry. The OTel trace_id does — it links the client error report directly to the server span.

antipattern.reason: This UUID is not correlated with any log line, span, or Sentry event. It creates a false sense of traceability.

rule.statement: Never put exception messages, stack traces, SQL queries, connection strings, file paths, or internal hostnames in the detail or errors[] fields. Scrub all user-supplied data of newlines and control characters to prevent log injection.
rule.rationale: Error detail is part of the API response — it reaches browsers, mobile apps, and third-party integrations. DSN strings, table names, and stack frames are confidential and create attack surface.

antipattern.reason: SQLAlchemy exceptions include the full query, table name, and constraint — all internal. Return a generic message and log the exception server-side with the traceId.

section.name: coverage-completeness

rule.statement: Reference ProblemDetail in BOTH 4xx AND 5xx responses for every OpenAPI operation. Run spectral or check_problem_refs.py in CI to enforce this.
rule.rationale: Agents add ProblemDetail to 4xx but forget 5xx. 500 responses without a ProblemDetail body break client SDK code-gen that expects a uniform error shape.

section.reference: CI script that validates every 4xx/5xx operation response references ProblemDetail.

rule.statement: Pin type URIs to a versioned path (/errors/v1/validation-error). Never rename or delete a type URI after it ships — that breaks clients who branch on the string.
rule.rationale: RFC 7807 type is a stable identifier, not a display string. Clients may hardcode it in switch/match statements. Version the path so renames introduce a new URI rather than breaking the old one.

section.name: limitations-and-tradeoffs
section.p: Validation errors don't fit RFC 7807 cleanly; the errors[] extension is non-standard and must be documented. type URIs that 404 are common in practice — keep them stable or move docs behind a permalink. Stack traces in detail leak in non-prod by accident; need an env-gated debug mode. Multi-error responses (batch endpoints) need errors[] with per-item statuses; status at top is then redundant. Localizing messages requires either Accept-Language handling server-side or codes-only payloads with client-side i18n. Some frameworks (Django REST default, Rails) ship their own error shape; integration requires a custom renderer.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Drive RFC 7807 adoption with an LLM by handing the agent the existing exception-handler/middleware file plus the OpenAPI spec; the agent (1) inventories existing error shapes, (2) defines ProblemDetail + FieldError schemas, (3) writes framework-specific exception handlers, (4) updates OpenAPI components and per-endpoint responses. Always run a regeneration step on the typed client SDK and a contract test pass to catch consumers that hard-coded the old shape. Trace-ID propagation should be wired through the logging middleware in the same task — otherwise the traceId in the body and the trace in the log won't match.

section.name: cli-tools
section.p: openapi-generator: Generate typed clients from updated Problem schema. schemathesis: Property-test that every response matches OpenAPI ProblemDetail. dredd: Contract-test API against OpenAPI/HAR. spectral: Lint OpenAPI for missing responses[4xx,5xx] refs. sentry-cli: Upload sourcemaps so trace IDs in Problem responses link to stack traces. httpx / curl -i: Manually verify Problem envelope on each endpoint.

section.name: services-and-tools
section.p: Sentry (SaaS + OSS): Ingest traceId; tag errors[] codes; alert on type frequency. Datadog APM (SaaS): Use dd.trace_id as traceId so logs+errors join. Grafana Tempo (OSS): Same idea via OTel trace_id. Stoplight Studio (SaaS): Edit Problem schema collaboratively; export OpenAPI. Postman (SaaS): Add tests asserting type/title/status/traceId on every response. Apigee / Kong (SaaS / OSS gateway): Wrap upstream errors in Problem at the edge for legacy services.

list.item: Pin the type URI to a versioned docs path (/errors/v1/validation-error) so renames don't break clients.
Generate traceId as the same value used by your tracing SDK (OTel trace_id), not a fresh UUID.
For validation errors, set code to a stable machine-friendly slug (invalid_format), keep message human and localizable.
Keep title short and the same for a given type; let detail carry the per-request specifics.
Return application/problem+json Content-Type, not application/json — proxies and clients can branch on it.
Add a 5xx response with traceId even on unhandled exceptions so support can find the trace without reproducing.
Document every error code in the same OpenAPI doc; agents should regenerate docs in the same PR as the handler change.
Strip newlines and control chars from any user-supplied data before placing it in detail to prevent log injection.

antipattern.description: Adding ProblemDetail only to 422 (validation) and omitting 401, 403, 404, 409, 500.
antipattern.reason: Every error status a client can receive needs the same envelope. Clients must not branch on Content-Type to detect whether an error is RFC 7807 or ad-hoc.

antipattern.description: Reusing a Python RFC 7807 pattern verbatim in Go or Java.
antipattern.reason: Each language has its own idioms: errors.As in Go, @ControllerAdvice in Spring, ProblemDetail.forRawStatusCode in Spring 6. Require per-language idiomatic implementations.

template.purpose: Pydantic ProblemDetail model + FastAPI exception handlers.
OpenAPI components/schemas for ProblemDetail and FieldError.
Script verifying every 4xx/5xx op refs ProblemDetail in OpenAPI spec.

script.purpose: Validates that every 4xx/5xx response in an OpenAPI doc points at ProblemDetail.
